title: Terraform Infrastructure-as-Code Gap Audit
scope: receptor-infra (primary) — Azure resources: Key Vault K3sUnlock, storage account k3sbackups71a475f1dae6, service principal vault-k3s-unseal; supabase-kubernetes production readiness; CI workflow coverage

findings.description: supabase/production/values.yaml contains literal shell interpolation placeholders ('${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}', '${JWT_SECRET}') with a comment 'Placeholder for real secrets — these should be injected via environment or Vault'. These are NOT resolved by Helm or VSO — they will render as literal strings in the Helm release, meaning Supabase is either failing to start or running with blank/broken credentials. supabase/staging/values.yaml omits secrets entirely. Neither environment has VSO VaultStaticSecret or VaultDynamicSecret CRDs declared for Supabase credential injection. This is the most critical gap for production readiness of supabase-kubernetes.

tasks.description: Store all Supabase secrets in Vault KV at: secret/supabase/production/{postgres-password,jwt-secret,anon-key,service-role-key,dashboard-password} and secret/supabase/staging/{...}. Create VaultStaticSecret CRDs at supabase/production/vso-secrets.yaml and supabase/staging/vso-secrets.yaml following the pattern of vault/vso-github-app-secret.yaml. Each CRD syncs one Vault KV path into a k8s Secret in the respective namespace (supabase / supabase-staging). refreshAfter: 1h.

Update supabase/production/values.yaml and supabase/staging/values.yaml to reference the VSO-synced k8s Secret via the chart's existingSecret pattern or direct envFrom.secretRef. Remove all ${...} placeholder strings. Verify with 'helm template supabase supabase/supabase -f supabase/production/values.yaml | grep -c "\${"' returns 0.

Create a VaultAuth CRD for the supabase namespace (vault/vso-supabase-auth.yaml) following the pattern of vault/vso-vault-auth.yaml. The Vault role for supabase must have read access to secret/data/supabase/production/* and be bound to sa-supabase in namespace supabase. Equivalent role and CRD for staging (secret/data/supabase/staging/*, sa-supabase in supabase-staging).

findings.description: The auto-unseal identity for HashiCorp Vault is an App Registration service principal ('vault-k3s-unseal', appId: aad24e26-8e10-4751-90ea-fb43bd147250) with a client secret (hint: y3I) expiring 2028-03-12. values/vault.yaml (confirmed via gh CLI) injects AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_ID, and AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET from a Kubernetes Secret named 'vault-azure-kms' via secretKeyRef — the secret is NOT stored in plaintext in the values file. However, 'vault-azure-kms' was created manually (kubectl create secret) with no VSO sync and no IaC tracking (see STORE-01). The client secret has no automated rotation mechanism. Expiry or revocation would seal the Vault cluster until a human manually creates a new secret and redeploys. Preferred replacement: Azure AD Workload Identity Federation bound to the Vault Kubernetes service account (eliminates the client secret entirely — no Arc enrollment required for on-premise k3s).

tasks.description: Evaluate Azure AD Workload Identity Federation for vault-k3s-unseal: configure federation on the vault-k3s-unseal App Registration, binding it to the Vault Kubernetes service account JWT (sa-vault in vault namespace). Update values/vault.yaml to use the federated identity (AZURE_CLIENT_ID + AZURE_TENANT_ID only, no AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET). On-premise k3s is supported as long as the OIDC issuer URL is reachable by Azure AD (expose via Cloudflare Tunnel or public endpoint).

Once Workload Identity Federation is confirmed working ('vault status' shows unsealed after pod restart), revoke the vault-k3s client secret via 'az ad app credential delete --id aad24e26-8e10-4751-90ea-fb43bd147250 --key-id <credential-id>'. Remove AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET from the vault-azure-kms k8s secret and from values/vault.yaml extraEnvironmentVars. Update Terraform to manage the service principal without a credential.

findings.description: No terraform/ directory exists. Azure Key Vault K3sUnlock, storage account k3sbackups71a475f1dae6, and service principal vault-k3s-unseal were provisioned manually 2026-03-13 to 2026-03-15. Live resource identifiers confirmed via az CLI: subscription 303d0b34-0b31-4302-a133-f1bd1e61f4b7, resource group Receptor, location australiasoutheast. Desired end state: terraform/ directory with modules for key-vault, backup-storage, and service-principal; state in k3sbackups71a475f1dae6 (dedicated tfstate container).

tasks.description: Create terraform/ directory structure: modules/key-vault/, modules/backup-storage/, modules/service-principal/, and root main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, providers.tf (azurerm with use_oidc=true, workload identity federation), and backend.tf (azurerm backend: storage_account_name=k3sbackups71a475f1dae6, container_name=tfstate, key=receptor/azure.tfstate, use_azuread_auth=true).

Create blob container 'tfstate' in k3sbackups71a475f1dae6 before terraform init (one-time bootstrap: 'az storage container create --name tfstate --account-name k3sbackups71a475f1dae6 --auth-mode login'). The receptor-backups container must not share state. Grant Storage Blob Data Contributor to the Terraform CI identity on this container only.

Write terraform/modules/key-vault/: azurerm_key_vault (name=K3sUnlock, sku=Standard, soft_delete_retention_days=90, purge_protection_enabled=true, public_network_access_enabled=true initially), azurerm_key_vault_key (name=vault-unseal, key_type=RSA, rotation_policy per SEC-03), and azurerm_key_vault_access_policy blocks. Import: 'terraform import azurerm_key_vault.k3sunlock /subscriptions/303d0b34-0b31-4302-a133-f1bd1e61f4b7/resourceGroups/Receptor/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/K3sUnlock'.

Write terraform/modules/backup-storage/: azurerm_storage_account (name=k3sbackups71a475f1dae6, account_replication_type=RAGRS — matching live state not ARM template, min_tls_version=TLS1_2, allow_nested_items_to_be_public=false, account_kind=StorageV2, access_tier=Cool, shared_access_key_enabled=true initially — see SEC-04 for hardening path). Import: 'terraform import azurerm_storage_account.backup /subscriptions/303d0b34-0b31-4302-a133-f1bd1e61f4b7/resourceGroups/Receptor/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/k3sbackups71a475f1dae6'.

Run 'terraform import' for all resources, then 'terraform plan'. Confirm plan shows zero changes for stable properties (name, sku, soft-delete, RAGRS) and proposed changes only for remediation items (KV network ACLs per SEC-01, key rotation per SEC-03). Document import commands in terraform/BOOTSTRAP.md.

findings.description: Azure Key Vault K3sUnlock (URI: https://k3sunlock.vault.azure.net/) and its access policies have no IaC representation. Live state confirmed via az CLI: Standard SKU, purge protection enabled, soft-delete 90 days, two access policies (admin objectId 24f68cfb-d4c3-4698-9d5f-91e724a8eee6 and unseal SP 26b134d5-deb6-4856-8c48-3d5f8b3c6262). The unseal key is 'vault-unseal' (RSA, enabled 2026-03-13, no expiry, no rotation policy). This finding is addressed by IAC-01-T3.

tasks.description: Resolved as part of IAC-01-T3 (Terraform module + import). Live state fully documented in this audit. No standalone action required.

findings.description: Key Vault K3sUnlock has networkAcls.defaultAction=Allow with zero IP rules and no virtual network rules (confirmed via az CLI). The vault URI https://k3sunlock.vault.azure.net/ is reachable from any public IP. The only caller requiring access is the Vault pod on the k3s cluster (via the vault-k3s-unseal service principal). Restricting to the Hyper-V host public egress IP would close this exposure without impacting the unseal flow.

tasks.description: Restricted public Key Vault access to TPG residential CIDRs (14 curated ranges). User IP verified within these ranges. Bypass set to 'AzureServices' to ensure unseal SP continues to work.

findings.description: The Kubernetes secret 'vault-azure-kms' in the vault namespace (referenced by values/vault.yaml secretKeyRef for AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET) was created manually via kubectl and has no VSO sync, no IaC declaration, and no automated rotation mechanism. Only 'github-app-credentials' is VSO-managed (vault/vso-github-app-secret.yaml). If the vault namespace is accidentally wiped, vault-azure-kms is lost and Vault cannot unseal until manually recreated. Remediation: create a VaultStaticSecret CRD to VSO-manage it, or eliminate it entirely via Workload Identity Federation (SEC-02).

tasks.description: Determine where vault-azure-kms is currently sourced: run 'vault kv get secret/infrastructure/azure-unseal'. If the path exists, create vault/vso-azure-kms-secret.yaml as a VaultStaticSecret CRD mirroring vault/vso-github-app-secret.yaml (kv-v2, path=infrastructure/azure-unseal, destination.name=vault-azure-kms, namespace=vault, refreshAfter=1h). If not in Vault KV, store it there first then create the CRD.

If SEC-02 Workload Identity Federation migration is implemented first, vault-azure-kms becomes unnecessary. Delete the k8s secret, remove the AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET extraEnvironmentVar from values/vault.yaml, and skip STORE-01-T1. This is the preferred path — it eliminates the secret rather than managing its lifecycle.

findings.description: Neither supabase/production/values.yaml nor supabase/staging/values.yaml declares resource requests or limits for any Supabase component (db, studio, kong, auth, rest, realtime, storage, meta). Per kubernetes-specialist skill: 'Deploy to production without resource limits — MUST NOT DO'. Without limits, a runaway Supabase component can starve Vault or ARC runners on the single-node k3s cluster, disrupting the entire ecosystem. The supabase-kubernetes Helm chart supports per-component resources blocks.

tasks.description: Add resource requests and limits for all enabled Supabase components in supabase/production/values.yaml. Baseline values (tune after observing real usage with 'kubectl top pods -n supabase'): db: requests cpu=250m mem=512Mi limits cpu=1000m mem=2Gi; studio: requests cpu=100m mem=256Mi limits cpu=500m mem=512Mi; kong: requests cpu=100m mem=256Mi limits cpu=500m mem=512Mi; auth/rest/meta: requests cpu=50m mem=128Mi limits cpu=250m mem=256Mi; realtime: requests cpu=100m mem=256Mi limits cpu=500m mem=512Mi.

Add proportionally smaller resource limits for supabase/staging/values.yaml (50% of production values). Add a ResourceQuota manifest to the supabase-staging namespace capping total CPU and memory to prevent staging from impacting production workloads on the same single-node cluster.

findings.description: No testing/ephemeral environment exists for supabase-kubernetes. helmfile.yaml declares only 'supabase' (production, namespace supabase) and 'supabase-staging' (namespace supabase-staging). There is no 'supabase-test' Helm release, no supabase/test/values.yaml, and no mechanism for ephemeral per-PR Supabase instances. The existing CI RBAC (sa-github-runner / ci-namespace-manager ClusterRole) grants permission to create reactor-ci-* namespaces, suggesting ephemeral environments were planned but not implemented for Supabase. Without a test environment, integration tests in supabase-receptor CI run against the shared staging instance, creating test isolation risks.

tasks.description: Created supabase/test/values.yaml with minimal resource footprint. Added supabase-test release to helmfile.yaml.
Created VaultStaticSecret for test credentials and updated VaultAuth (vso-supabase-auth.yaml) for the test namespace.
Created ADR-010-supabase-environments.md documenting decision for static test namespace due to cluster capacity constraints.

findings.description: azure/backup-storage-account.parameters.json has three discrepancies from live state: (1) storageAccountName: <SET_ME> vs live k3sbackups71a475f1dae6; (2) accountType: Standard_LRS vs live Standard_RAGRS; (3) location: australiaeast vs live australiasoutheast. The ARM template was never applied and does not reflect reality on two configuration fields in addition to the missing name. Retire the azure/ directory once Terraform import is confirmed.

tasks.description: After Terraform import of the storage account is confirmed (IAC-01-T4/T5), delete the azure/ directory from receptor-infra ('git rm -r azure/'). Update README.md to remove the ARM template reference and replace with a pointer to terraform/. Commit on a feature branch.

findings.description: No drift detection exists. The SKU discrepancy (Standard_LRS in ARM vs Standard_RAGRS live) and location discrepancy were invisible until az CLI inspection during this audit. A scheduled terraform plan run will surface future drift automatically.

tasks.description: Create .github/workflows/terraform-drift.yml: scheduled weekly (cron: '0 2 * * 1'), runs terraform plan -detailed-exitcode on arc-runner-receptor-infra. Exit code 2 (changes detected) fails the job and triggers Slack notification via the existing OIDC→Vault→Slack pattern from cluster-sync.yml. Implement in Phase 5 after apply workflow is proven.

findings.description: No terraform-plan.yml or terraform-apply.yml CI workflow exists. Any Terraform change today requires local az login and manual apply with no peer review gate and no audit trail. The cluster-sync.yml OIDC JWT/Vault pattern is the proven blueprint.

tasks.description: Create .github/workflows/terraform-plan.yml: trigger on pull_request (paths: terraform/**). Steps: checkout, setup-terraform, OIDC→Vault JWT exchange (reuse pattern from cluster-sync.yml), retrieve Azure Workload Identity credentials from Vault KV secret/infrastructure/azure-terraform, terraform init (AzureRM backend, use_azuread_auth=true), terraform validate, terraform plan. Post plan output as PR comment. Runner: arc-runner-receptor-infra.

Create .github/workflows/terraform-apply.yml: trigger on push to main (paths: terraform/**). Steps identical to plan workflow with final step 'terraform apply -auto-approve'. No AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET in GitHub Secrets — all credentials from Vault KV via OIDC JWT.

findings.description: No Vault JWT role or KV path exists for Azure credentials in a Terraform CI context. The receptor-infra-ci Vault role only grants access to secret/infrastructure/slack-incidents-webhook. A dedicated JWT role (receptor-infra-tf-ci) and policy must be created. The KV secret should store only client_id and tenant_id (no client_secret) if SEC-02 Workload Identity Federation is implemented first.

tasks.description: Create Vault KV secret at secret/infrastructure/azure-terraform containing: client_id (vault-k3s-unseal appId: aad24e26-8e10-4751-90ea-fb43bd147250 or future federated identity), tenant_id (76c68bd9-6fba-42d1-bf81-471e2e8c1395), subscription_id (303d0b34-0b31-4302-a133-f1bd1e61f4b7). No client_secret if Workload Identity Federation is used.

Create Vault policy receptor-infra-tf with read access to secret/data/infrastructure/azure-terraform. Create Vault JWT role receptor-infra-tf-ci bound to this policy, scoped to repo dm-ra-01/receptor-infra (bound_claims: sub=repo:dm-ra-01/receptor-infra:*). Document in supabase-receptor vault-configuration.md.

findings.description: The unseal key 'vault-unseal' in Key Vault K3sUnlock has rotationPolicy=null (confirmed via az keyvault key show). No automatic rotation schedule exists. Azure Key Vault supports managed rotation policies. A Terraform azurerm_key_vault_key rotation_policy block should declare an annual rotation cadence with a 30-day pre-rotation notification. RSA wrapKey/unwrapKey is version-agnostic so rotation is safe for Vault auto-unseal.

tasks.description: Add a rotation_policy block to the azurerm_key_vault_key resource in terraform/modules/key-vault/: time_after_creation='P1Y' (rotate annually), notify='P30D'. Apply via terraform-apply.yml (Phase 5). Confirm via 'az keyvault key rotation-policy show --vault-name K3sUnlock --name vault-unseal' after apply.

findings.description: Storage account k3sbackups71a475f1dae6 has allowSharedKeyAccess=true with keyExpirationPeriodInDays=60 (confirmed via az CLI). Shared key access allows callers with the account key to bypass Azure AD authorization. The Terraform AzureRM backend must use use_azuread_auth=true; once all storage consumers are confirmed on Azure AD auth, allowSharedKeyAccess can be set to false.

tasks.description: In terraform/backend.tf, set use_azuread_auth=true on the azurerm backend block. Grant 'Storage Blob Data Contributor' role to the Terraform CI identity on the tfstate container only: 'az role assignment create --role "Storage Blob Data Contributor" --assignee <ci-identity-object-id> --scope /subscriptions/303d0b34-0b31-4302-a133-f1bd1e61f4b7/resourceGroups/Receptor/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/k3sbackups71a475f1dae6/blobServices/default/containers/tfstate'. Verify 'terraform init' and 'terraform plan' succeed before proceeding.

BLOCKED: Pre-flight audit revealed that rclone backup scripts in supabase-receptor rely on shared key authentication ('account_key' from Vault). shared_access_key_enabled must remain true until scripts are migrated to SAS or managed identity.

findings.description: Neither supabase/production/values.yaml nor supabase/staging/values.yaml declares podSecurityContext or containerSecurityContext overrides. By default the supabase-kubernetes Helm chart may run containers as root or with allowPrivilegeEscalation=true. Per kubernetes-specialist skill: 'Run containers as root without justification — MUST NOT DO'. The PostgreSQL container legitimately requires a specific UID/GID; all other components (studio, kong, auth, rest, realtime) should run non-root.

tasks.description: Added securityContext overrides to all Supabase core services (prod, staging, test) enforcing runAsNonRoot, allowPrivilegeEscalation=false, and dropping all capabilities (except DB which uses UID 999).

findings.description: Falco (runtime security monitoring) is commented out in helmfile.yaml with no explanation. The falco.yaml values file and sa-falco ServiceAccount/RBAC are present, indicating Falco was planned. Without Falco, there is no runtime threat detection for the supabase namespace (unexpected process execution in Postgres container, shell spawned from auth service, unexpected outbound connections). This is important for a production Supabase instance holding patient/staff data.

tasks.description: Re-enabled Falco in helmfile.yaml and switched to Custom Rules management.
Added supabase-specific custom Falco rules and updated Vault egress rules to permit Azure KV traffic.

findings.description: azure/backup-storage-account.parameters.json has three value discrepancies from live state (storageAccountName placeholder, accountType Standard_LRS vs live Standard_RAGRS, location australiaeast vs live australiasoutheast). Superseded by IAC-02 — tracked here for severity summary completeness.

tasks.description: Resolved as part of IAC-02-T1 (delete azure/ directory after Terraform import). No standalone fix required.

findings.description: k3s/helmfile.yaml (supabase-receptor) references values files at k3s/values/calico.yaml, k3s/values/cert-manager.yaml, k3s/values/traefik.yaml, k3s/values/vault.yaml, k3s/values/prometheus-stack.yaml, k3s/values/loki.yaml, k3s/values/falco.yaml, and k3s/values/cloudflared.yaml — but the k3s/values/ directory does not exist on disk. Running 'helmfile sync' from supabase-receptor would fail immediately with file-not-found errors for all 8 chart values files. The helmfile documents the intended chart list and dependency order but cannot be applied as written.

tasks.description: Create k3s/values/ directory and populate the 8 missing values files: calico.yaml, cert-manager.yaml, traefik.yaml, vault.yaml, prometheus-stack.yaml, loki.yaml, falco.yaml, cloudflared.yaml. For vault.yaml, mirror the structure of receptor-infra/values/vault.yaml. For falco.yaml, reference k3s/falco/falco-rules.yaml as customRules. For cloudflared.yaml, inject the tunnel token from Vault KV. Run 'helmfile lint' and 'helmfile diff --dry-run' to confirm all values files resolve.

findings.description: The vault namespace NetworkPolicy in k3s/network-policies/network-policies.yaml restricts all egress to DNS only (port 53 to kube-system). However, Vault's Azure Key Vault auto-unseal (SEC-02) requires outbound HTTPS (port 443) to https://k3sunlock.vault.azure.net/ to perform wrapKey/unwrapKey operations. The current egress policy silently blocks every auto-unseal attempt — Vault will remain sealed after any pod restart. This is a latent risk: the cluster appears healthy until Vault is restarted, at which point the sealed state prevents all VSO secret injection (including KUBE-01).

tasks.description: Add an egress rule to the vault namespace NetworkPolicy allowing outbound port 443 to the Azure Key Vault endpoint (k3sunlock.vault.azure.net). Use an ipBlock cidr covering Azure Key Vault IPs for australiasoutheast, or configure an FQDN-based egress policy if Calico GlobalNetworkPolicy is available. Verify by restarting the Vault pod and confirming 'vault status' reports Initialized=true, Sealed=false.

findings.description: The k3s cluster is defined across two separate repos: supabase-receptor/k3s/helmfile.yaml (Calico, cert-manager, Traefik, Vault, Prometheus, Loki, Falco, cloudflared) and receptor-infra/helmfile.yaml (supabase + supabase-staging, VSO CRDs, network policies). There is no top-level umbrella helmfile, no documented apply order, and no CI that validates both helmfiles together. An operator must know to apply supabase-receptor first then receptor-infra, and that Vault from supabase-receptor is a prerequisite for VSO in receptor-infra. The supabase-receptor helm-upgrade-check.yml only scans its own helmfile — it misses the supabase-community/supabase chart pinned in receptor-infra.

tasks.description: Document the two-repo helmfile split in docs/adr/ADR-009-helmfile-split.md with the canonical apply order: (1) supabase-receptor/k3s/helmfile.yaml, (2) receptor-infra/helmfile.yaml. Include cross-repo dependency notes (Vault from repo 1 is required before Supabase in repo 2). Link from both repos' README.md cluster-setup sections.

Create a root-level cluster-apply.sh in receptor-infra (or supabase-receptor) that drives both helmfile syncs in order with --wait between phases. Extend helm-upgrade-check.yml to also check chart versions from receptor-infra/helmfile.yaml so both helmfiles are covered by the quarterly review.

findings.description: staging-smoke.yml runs on 'ubuntu-latest' (GitHub-hosted runner). The workflow's inline comment explicitly states: 'On GitHub-hosted runners (ubuntu-latest), this step will fail because the internal Vault endpoint is not reachable.' The Vault OIDC login step therefore fails on every push to main, breaking post-deployment verification. The self-hosted arc-runner is commented out pending a '7-run trial gate' (ARCH-03 from 260312-cicd-environments audit) that has not been activated. The staging smoke test has been silently broken since it was written.

tasks.description: Check whether the ARCH-03 7-run trial gate from 260312-cicd-environments has been met. If met: uncomment 'runs-on: [self-hosted, k3s]' and comment out 'ubuntu-latest' in staging-smoke.yml. If not met: document the remaining count and continue the trial with ci.yml first. Confirm the arc-runner-receptor-infra runner is registered and labelled 'k3s' before switching.

findings.description: The ci-namespace-manager ClusterRole (k3s/rbac/serviceaccounts.yaml) grants verbs: ['*'] on secrets, pods, services, configmaps, persistentvolumeclaims, deployments, statefulsets, and replicasets — cluster-wide. The intended scope is reactor-ci-* namespaces only, but the ClusterRoleBinding makes it cluster-scoped. The inline comment acknowledges that 'a future Kyverno/OPA Gatekeeper admission webhook will enforce the receptor-ci-* prefix programmatically (ADR-007)' — but no admission controller has been deployed or planned. Until it is, a compromised sa-github-runner has write access to secrets and pods in vault and supabase namespaces.

tasks.description: Deploy Kyverno (lighter weight than OPA Gatekeeper on single-node k3s) via a new Helm release in supabase-receptor/k3s/helmfile.yaml: kyverno/kyverno (pinned stable version). Create a Kyverno ClusterPolicy that validates namespace create/delete requests from sa-github-runner: allow only namespaces matching '^reactor-ci-'. Until Kyverno is deployed, update docs/adr/ADR-007-kubernetes-rbac.md to explicitly document the open risk and expected remediation date.

findings.description: The cluster currently relies on the local-path-provisioner for all persistent storage (Supabase, Vault). This binds PVCs to the physical hardware of a single k3s node. In the current multi-host architecture (SPACESHIP and ENTERPRISE), this prevents highly available failover of stateful workloads. If a host fails, its local-path data is inaccessible until the host is restored. Distribution of storage with replication (e.g., Longhorn) is required to ensure data redundancy across physical failure domains.

tasks.description: Create 'longhorn-backups' container in storage account k3sbackups71a475f1dae6 via Terraform (modules/backup-storage/main.tf). This will serve as the off-cluster backup target for Longhorn snapshots.
Add Longhorn release to receptor-infra/helmfile.yaml. Configuration should use the 'longhorn' namespace. Use version pinning for the longhorn/longhorn chart.

Create receptor-infra/values/longhorn.yaml. Configure defaultDataPath, replication factor (3 for HA), and backupTarget (pointing to the Azure container created in T1). Ensure it references the appropriate secret for Azure storage account key access.

Update supabase/production/values.yaml to use 'longhorn' as the persistence.storageClass for the database. Note: migration of existing data from local-path to longhorn will require a backup/restore cycle or volume-migration-tooling.

findings.description: No deployment workflow exists in any of the three Next.js frontend repositories (planner-frontend, preference-frontend, workforce-frontend). Each has a ci.yml covering lint/unit/integration/build/codegen/e2e — but there is no deploy.yml, no staging-deploy.yml, and no production-deploy.yml. Merging a PR to main has no deployment consequence; the application is never updated on any environment by any automated mechanism. There are no staging URLs documented, no post-deploy health checks, and no rollback mechanism. The three frontends exist in production limbo: thoroughly tested in CI but never deployed.

tasks.description: Create Dockerfile (Next.js standalone output: output: standalone in next.config.ts) for each frontend repo. Add build-push.yml: on push to main, build the Docker image and push to GHCR (ghcr.io/dm-ra-01/<repo>:<sha>). Create k8s Deployment and Service manifests in receptor-infra under infrastructure/<app>/ namespace (planner, preference, workforce). Add Traefik IngressRoute per app. Add staging-deploy.yml: resolve staging Vault KV (infrastructure/<app>/staging), inject NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL + PUBLISHABLE_KEY, apply updated image tag via kubectl set image or helmfile, run smoke test. Wire Prometheus ServiceMonitor and Loki log scraping using existing monitoring stack.

Create prod-deploy.yml for each frontend triggered on GitHub Release publish. Deploy to production k8s namespace. Must include: (1) GitHub Environments required-reviewer gate (CICD-08), (2) production Vault KV injection (infrastructure/<app>/prod), (3) post-deploy smoke test against production Traefik URL, (4) automatic rollback (kubectl rollout undo) on smoke test failure within 5 minutes. Blocked on LIFE-04 (rollback runbook).

findings.description: match-backend has a Dockerfile and a full CI pipeline (unit + integration tests) but no image build/push workflow and no deployment workflow. The Docker image is never pushed to any container registry (no GHCR or Azure Container Registry config exists). The Dockerfile entrypoint is the allocator CLI — it is undocumented whether match-backend runs as a persistent HTTP service or a batch job in production. No k8s Deployment, CronJob, or Job manifest exists for match-backend in receptor-infra.

tasks.description: Write ADR-010-match-backend-runtime.md: always-on FastAPI/uvicorn HTTP service. Create Dockerfile (FROM python:3.12-slim-bookworm, expose port 8080, ENTRYPOINT uvicorn allocator.main:app). Add build-push.yml targeting GHCR. Create k8s Deployment in receptor-infra with: VSO for INTERNAL_API_KEY injection, liveness probe (GET /healthz), readiness probe (GET /readyz), resource limits (250m CPU, 512Mi memory). Add Traefik IngressRoute for internal-only access (no public exposure). Future: add POST /run webhook endpoint so Supabase Edge Function can trigger a match run on demand without polling.

findings.description: receptor-planner CI only runs unit tests. The ci.yml comment defers integration tests indefinitely: 'Integration tests (if added later) live in tests/integration/' — with no tracking task, unlike match-backend which has BACK-01-T2. receptor-planner also has no Dockerfile, no container image strategy, and no deployment workflow. It is unknown how the planner service is invoked in staging/production — whether as a long-running process, a cronjob, or an on-demand CLI call from the arc-runner.

tasks.description: Document receptor-planner runtime model in ADR-011-receptor-planner-runtime.md. Create tests/integration/ with at least one integration test verifying the planner reads from and writes back to schedule_slots in a local Supabase instance. If receptor-planner runs as a container: add a Dockerfile (mirror match-backend pattern) and build-push.yml. Add integration tests to ci.yml mirroring match-backend's BACK-01-T2 pattern.

findings.description: No rollback procedure exists — automated or documented — for any of the five frontend/backend repositories. There are no GitHub Release rollback runbooks, no kubectl rollout undo automations, no pinned image digests in k8s manifests (those do not exist yet per LIFE-01/02/03), and no Alertmanager alert that tracks post-deployment error-rate regression. The only recovery after a bad deployment would be a manual git revert and re-run of a deploy workflow that does not yet exist.

tasks.description: Create docs/operations/rollback-runbook.md covering: (1) frontend rollback — force-push previous image tag or re-trigger staging-deploy.yml from a previous release tag; (2) backend rollback — kubectl rollout undo Deployment/<service>; (3) database migration rollback policy — migrations are forward-only in Supabase, so define the compensating-migration strategy. Add an Alertmanager PrometheusRule that fires a 'DeploymentErrorRateSpike' alert within 5 minutes of deployment if HTTP 5xx rate exceeds baseline. Link rollback runbook from all five repo READMEs.

findings.description: The three Next.js frontend CI pipelines all run integration tests against a locally booted Supabase instance but have no per-environment configuration for staging vs. production. No NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL is configured per environment, no Vault KV paths for frontend staging/prod secrets exist, and no staging health-check gate verifies the live staging Supabase before deploying. CI tests pass against an isolated local schema while staging/production use different Supabase configs that are never exercised by CI.

tasks.description: Add Vault KV paths for each frontend per environment: infrastructure/planner-frontend/staging, infrastructure/planner-frontend/prod (and equivalents for preference/workforce). Each path contains NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL and NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY for that environment. Update staging-deploy.yml (LIFE-01-T1) to inject these via the Vault OIDC login step. Ensure next.config.ts runtime env vars are not baked at build time — use NEXT_PUBLIC_ env vars injected at runtime via k8s ConfigMap or Cloudflare Pages variable.

findings.description: receptor-planner has no dependency management automation: no Renovate Bot (.github/workflows/renovate.yml) unlike the three frontend repos, and no Dependabot (.github/dependabot.yml) unlike match-backend. requirements.txt includes ortools (~200 MB), scipy, and other scientific packages pinned without automated update tracking. Untracked dependency drift exposes security vulnerabilities and silent incompatibilities when the k3s node Python version advances.

tasks.description: Add .github/dependabot.yml to receptor-planner targeting pip (package-ecosystem: pip, directory: /, schedule: monthly — matching match-backend). Ensure the TMPDIR: /home/runner/pip-tmp workaround in ci.yml is preserved after any pip upgrade that bumps ortools.

findings.description: All three Next.js frontend next.config.ts files include script-src: 'unsafe-eval' 'unsafe-inline' in the Content-Security-Policy header. 'unsafe-eval' permits arbitrary eval() and Function() execution — defeating XSS protections for the entire application. The inline comment acknowledges this: 'tighten with nonces in future' — but no ADR, milestone, or tracking item exists. All three frontends are deployed to production (when deployed) with this CSP weakness, affecting medical staff, workers, and administrators.

tasks.description: Replace 'unsafe-eval' with nonce-based CSP in all three frontend next.config.ts files. Next.js 14+ supports automatic nonce injection via middleware.ts and the experimental.nonce config option. Add a CSP header verification test to the e2e-axe Playwright suite that asserts the Content-Security-Policy response header does not contain 'unsafe-eval'. Until nonces are implemented, add the known CSP risk to the ISMS Asset Register for all three applications.

clarifications.decision: No long-lived Azure credentials in GitHub Secrets. Azure Workload Identity Federation via OIDC JWT. GitHub OIDC token exchanged for short-lived Vault token (existing receptor-infra-ci pattern), then Azure client_id/tenant_id retrieved from Vault KV. azurerm provider uses use_oidc=true.

State stored in k3sbackups71a475f1dae6 in a NEW dedicated container named 'tfstate' (NOT the existing receptor-backups container). Created as one-time bootstrap before terraform init. Backend uses use_azuread_auth=true.
The azure/ ARM template directory should be deleted (git rm -r azure/) once Terraform successfully imports and manages both resources.

Terraform manages Azure resources only: Key Vault K3sUnlock, storage account k3sbackups71a475f1dae6, and the vault-k3s-unseal service principal. k3s cluster manifests remain in helmfile.yaml / Kubernetes YAML.

clarifications.item: Live Azure resource details (confirmed via az CLI)
clarifications.decision: Subscription: 303d0b34-0b31-4302-a133-f1bd1e61f4b7. Resource group: Receptor. Location: australiasoutheast. Key Vault: K3sUnlock (URI: https://k3sunlock.vault.azure.net/). Storage account: k3sbackups71a475f1dae6 (SKU: Standard_RAGRS). Auto-unseal SP: vault-k3s-unseal (appId: aad24e26-8e10-4751-90ea-fb43bd147250, objectId: 26b134d5-deb6-4856-8c48-3d5f8b3c6262). Tenant: 76c68bd9-6fba-42d1-bf81-471e2e8c1395. Unseal key: vault-unseal (RSA, no expiry, no rotation policy).

k3s VMs run on on-premise Hyper-V. No native Azure VM Managed Identity unless Arc-enrolled. Preferred SEC-02 remediation: Azure AD Workload Identity Federation bound to the Vault Kubernetes SA (sa-vault in vault namespace) — eliminates client secret, no Arc enrollment required.

Confirmed Critical. vault.yaml uses secretKeyRef from vault-azure-kms k8s Secret (NOT plaintext values file). vault-azure-kms has no VSO sync (STORE-01). Preferred fix is Workload Identity Federation (SEC-02-T1) which eliminates the client secret entirely.

SEC-04-T2 (disabling allowSharedKeyAccess) must NOT be applied until ALL consumers of the storage account have been confirmed to use Azure AD auth. SEC-04-T1 (use_azuread_auth=true on backend) must succeed in CI first. Pre-flight consumer audit is mandatory.

Production values.yaml has literal '${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}' and '${JWT_SECRET}' strings — not resolved by Helm or VSO. Production Supabase is either non-functional or running with empty credentials. Must be fixed before Phase 6 can progress. Staging has no secret declarations at all.

helmfile.yaml pins supabase-community/supabase chart at v0.5.1 for both production and staging. Implementation agents must check the chart's existingSecret pattern at https://github.com/supabase-community/supabase-kubernetes/blob/main/charts/supabase/README.md before implementing K8S-01-T2.

All namespaces (supabase, supabase-staging, supabase-test, vault, monitoring, arc-runners) compete for the same physical resources on one Hyper-V host. Resource limits (K8S-02) and ResourceQuotas on staging/test namespaces are essential to prevent those environments from starving production Vault and Supabase.
Pending human decision on whether to consolidate both helmfiles into receptor-infra or keep the current split. Until decided, ADR-009 must document the apply order so operators are not surprised.
Implementation agent must check whether the 7-run self-hosted runner trial referenced in 260312-cicd-environments (ARCH-03) has been satisfied before activating arc-runner in staging-smoke.yml.

DECIDED: k3s containerised Next.js with observability. Each frontend gets a Dockerfile (standalone output mode), a GHCR image push on merge to main, and a k8s Deployment in receptor-infra (own namespace per app — planner, preference, workforce). Traefik IngressRoute per app. Prometheus/Loki scraping via existing monitoring stack. Production deploy gated behind GitHub Environments required-reviewer (CICD-08). This replaces the original Cloudflare Pages intent.

DECIDED: always-on HTTP service (FastAPI/uvicorn). Runs as a k8s Deployment in receptor-infra. Future plan: expose a webhook endpoint so the orchestrator (e.g. Supabase Edge Function) can trigger a match run on demand. For now the service is always running and polled. VSO injects INTERNAL_API_KEY. Liveness/readiness probes required (ADR-010).

implementationPhases.name: Credential Safety and CI Prerequisites
implementationPhases.rationale: Before any Terraform or CI work: VSO-manage vault-azure-kms or migrate to Workload Identity Federation (SEC-02, STORE-01); create Vault JWT role and KV path for Terraform CI credentials (CI-02).

implementationPhases.name: Terraform Modules and State Bootstrap
implementationPhases.rationale: Create tfstate container, write Terraform modules for Key Vault and storage account, run terraform import, confirm zero-drift plan.

implementationPhases.name: CI Workflow and ARM Retirement
implementationPhases.rationale: Implement terraform-plan.yml and terraform-apply.yml. Delete azure/ ARM directory after apply workflow proven.

implementationPhases.name: Storage AD Auth and Shared Key Removal
implementationPhases.rationale: Configure use_azuread_auth=true on Terraform backend. After consumer pre-flight audit, disable allowSharedKeyAccess.

implementationPhases.name: Azure Security Hardening and Drift Detection
implementationPhases.rationale: Apply KV network ACL restriction and key rotation policy via the apply workflow. Add weekly drift detection. Confirm ARM-01 retirement.

implementationPhases.name: Supabase-Kubernetes Production Readiness
implementationPhases.rationale: Address supabase-kubernetes gaps in priority order: (1) K8S-01 VSO secret injection — blocking for production functionality; (2) K8S-02 resource limits — blocking for cluster stability; (3) ENV-01 test environment — required for CI isolation; (4) K8S-03 pod security contexts — hardening; (5) K8S-04 Falco — runtime monitoring. K8S-01 and K8S-02 may be implemented concurrently.

implementationPhases.name: supabase-receptor k3s Completeness and RBAC Hardening
implementationPhases.rationale: supabase-receptor k3s/ infrastructure gaps: (1) NET-01 Vault egress fix — needed for auto-unseal to function; (2) ARCH-01 missing values files — helmfile is not apply-able; (3) CI-03 activate self-hosted runner for staging smoke; (4) SPLIT-01 document or consolidate two-repo helmfile split; (5) RBAC-01 deploy Kyverno to enforce namespace prefix policy.

implementationPhases.name: Frontend and Backend Lifecycle Management
implementationPhases.rationale: All five repos lack deployment automation. Priority: (1) LIFE-01 frontend staging deploy — blocks all staging verification; (2) LIFE-04 rollback runbook — prerequisite for any production deploy; (3) LIFE-02/03 backend ADRs — unblock container/deploy strategy decisions; (4) LIFE-05 per-environment Vault secrets; (5) LIFE-07 CSP hardening — security regression in production today; (6) LIFE-06 receptor-planner Dependabot — lowest risk.

implementationPhases.name: High-Availability Storage (Longhorn)
implementationPhases.rationale: Implement replicated block storage across SPACESHIP and ENTERPRISE hosts to eliminate single-point-of-failure for stateful workloads (Supabase, Vault).
